title: Creating and Probing Spin-Squeezed States of Molecules
authors: Connor M. Holland, Callum L. Welsh, Yukai Lu, David Wellnitz, Xing-Yan Chen, Ana Maria Rey, Lawrence W. Cheuk
abstract: Polar molecules are a promising platform for quantum-enhanced sensing and precision tests of fundamental physics, owing to their strong long-range dipolar interactions, broad sensitivity to electromagnetic fields, and sensitivity to potential physics beyond the Standard Model. However, the creation of metrologically useful entangled states in molecular systems has remained elusive. Here, we report the first observation of a class of metrologically useful entangled states - spin-squeezed states - in polar CaF molecules trapped in an optical tweezer array. The spin degree of freedom is encoded in rotational levels which are directly coupled by dipolar exchange interactions. By harnessing appropriate dynamical decoupling schemes we observe up to 3.0(3)dB of metrological gain, (2.2(3)dB without measurement correction) from direct exchange interactions. Using Floquet engineering, we further realize richer Hamiltonians that preserve spin squeezing while enabling the development of longer-range quantum correlations. Using site- and spin-resolved measurements we demonstrate that these entangled states enhance sensitivity to both homogeneous and spatially varying fields, and reveal strong non-classical correlations, including bipartite entanglement and Einstein-Podolsky-Rosen steering. Finally, we transfer the spin-squeezed states into long-lived and non-interacting hyperfine states, where the metrological enhancement persists for up to 100ms. Our results establish molecular optical tweezer arrays as a scalable platform for generating, controlling, characterizing, and storing entangled states of molecules, opening new opportunities for quantum-enhanced sensing and precision tests of fundamental physics.
summary: This paper reports the first observation of spin-squeezed states in polar CaF molecules trapped in optical tweezers. The researchers use engineered dipolar interactions to generate entanglement and demonstrate enhanced sensitivity for quantum sensing. Crucially, they show this quantum advantage can be preserved for long periods by storing the state in robust, non-interacting hyperfine levels.
main_problem: The primary goal is to create and probe metrologically useful entangled states, specifically spin-squeezed states, in molecular systems for quantum-enhanced sensing.
main_result: The authors successfully observed spin-squeezed states in polar CaF molecules and demonstrated that the metrological enhancement persists for up to 100ms even after transferring the state to long-lived hyperfine levels.
method: The study uses optical tweezer arrays to trap molecules, employing dynamical decoupling and Floquet engineering to generate and characterize entanglement, followed by site- and spin-resolved measurements.
model_or_system: The physical system is polar CaF molecules trapped in a 1D optical tweezer array. The spin degree of freedom is encoded in rotational levels, which interact via dipolar exchange interactions, modeled by an effective XXZ Hamiltonian.
key_observables: Wineland squeezing parameter ($\xi^2_W$), bipartite entanglement witness ($W$), EPR steering witness ($S_{A	o B}$), and the decay of metrological gain over time.
important_parameters: Metrological gain up to 3.0(3) dB; storage time up to 100ms; interaction strength ($\Delta$ parameter in XXZ model); noise parameters ($\sigma$ for global noise).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on the ability to perform site- and spin-resolved readout, and later sections model decoherence using Lindblad master equations and Gaussian noise approximations.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the experimental setup, the dynamics of spin squeezing under XXZ interactions, the quantification of entanglement and EPR steering, and the decay of metrological enhancement over storage time.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from establishing the physical platform (trapping/loading) to generating the entangled state via engineered Hamiltonians (Floquet engineering), characterizing the entanglement (bipartite entanglement, EPR steering), and finally demonstrating the long-term storage of the quantum enhancement in non-interacting hyperfine states.
why_interesting: This work provides a concrete, scalable platform (molecular optical tweezer arrays) for generating and controlling complex quantum states, directly advancing the field of quantum sensing by demonstrating measurable metrological gains beyond the standard quantum limit.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Spin-Squeezing in a CaF Molecular Tweezer Array. (a) 1/r3 XXZ models are realized using Floquet engineering via periodic sequences of global microwave (MW) pulses. (b) CaF rotational qubit encoding. (c) Sequence for dynamically creating spin-squeezed states. Molecules are initialized along the y-axis of the collective Bloch sphere via a π/2 pulse about x, after which OAT-like dynamics shear the quantum state and produce squeezing. Rotation pulses applied after squeez- ing re-orient the state before spin-resolved population detec- tion, which measures ˆSz. Specifically, the spin noise Var[ ˆSθ] is measured after a rotation of angle θ about the y-axis, while the spin length requires...

FIG. 2. XX Squeezing Dynamics and Metrological Perfor- mance. (a) Normalized variance of ˆSnθ versus orientation angle θ, measured after 4.55 ms of XX squeezing. (b) Nor- malized variance of ˆSz versus interaction time t at θ∗. (c) Normalized spin length |⟨ˆSy⟩| versus interaction time t. (d) Wineland parameter ξ2 W versus squeezing duration t at θ∗. (e) ξ2 W versus readout angle θ. Insets show the spin-spin correla- tors ⟨Si zSj z⟩c at θ = 38◦(left) and θ = 120◦(right), revealing predominantly short-ranged correlations. (f) The staggered Wineland parameter ¯ξ2 W versus readout angle θ. Inset: illus- tration of a spatially staggered magnetic field with alternating sign on even and odd sites...

FIG. 3. Metrological Gain and Correlations for XXZ Squeez- ing. (a) Wineland parameter ξ2 W (t∗, θ∗) at the ideal squeezing optimum versus XXZ anisotropy ∆. (b) ξ2 W (t, θ∗) versus in- teraction time t for ∆= 0.8. For (a,b), the dashed line shows unitary dynamics from an ideal 1/r3 XXZ model. The shaded band shows the predictions of our noise model. (c) Connected spin-spin correlators ⟨Si zSj z⟩c measured at t∗for different ∆ (upper-right: experiment; lower-left: theory). The diagonals are not shown. (d) The mean spin-spin correlator Cz(r) for ∆= 0 versus t, where r is the spin separation. (e) Cz(r) versus t for ∆= 0.8. (f) Cz(r) versus ∆, revealing extended correlations near the Heisenberg...

FIG. 4. Bipartite Entanglement, EPR Steering, and Storage of Spin-Squeezed States. (a) Separability witness W versus ∆. (b) EPR steering witness S versus ∆(red: A→B steering; blue: B→A steering). (c) Hyperfine qubit encoding |0⟩-|1⟩. (d) Squeezing and transduction protocol. Molecules in the ro- tational encoding (|↑⟩-|↓⟩) are squeezed, transferred into the non-interacting hyperfine encoding [25], and stored. For read- out, populations are transferred back to the rotational encod- ing for spin-resolved detection. (e) Wineland parameter ξ2 W versus storage time t. Red (blue) shows data for an unentan- gled coherent spin state (spin-squeezed state). (f) Practical metrological advantage of the...

FIG. 1. SRI Sequence and Histograms: (a) SRI begins with molecules in the rotational encoding. Application of N12 and a MW π-pulse transfers |↑⟩→N = 3 and |↓⟩→|↑⟩. SRI-1 is applied to identify molecules originally in |↓⟩. Next, N32 is applied to return N = 3 →N = 1 before the SRI-2 image, which identifies molecules originally in |↑⟩. (b,c) Fluorescence histograms from the two images with molecules primarily initialized in either |↓⟩(b) or |↑⟩(c) and detection thresholds (red dashed).
